coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots/Fe2(MoO4)3 nanocomposites on Ni form as a efficient (pre)catalyst for OER and HER

coredata.dc:description: In this study, FeMoO4 nanoarray and small-sized NGQDs were effectively loaded onto nickel foam via a hydrothermal method, serving as a bifunctional catalyst. We optimized the activities of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) by comparing different quantities of quantum dots, finally identifying an appropriate ratio. Notably, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 demonstrated superior performance in alkaline electrolytes, requiring only 136 and 179 mV overpotential to attain 10 mA cm−2 for HER and OER, respectively. The double electrode composed of this material achieved a voltage of merely 1.57 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The experimental results indicate that Fe3+ serves as the active species for OER, while Mo5+ and oxygen defects on the catalyst surface play a key role in HER. XPS analysis further reveals that the recombination of graphene quantum dots increases the concentration of Fe3+, Mo5+, and oxygen defects. Thus, the recombination of graphene quantum dots is crucial for enhancing catalytic performance. This study offers a novel insight into the design of high-performance bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/Fe2(MoO4)3 nanocomposites on Ni form as a efficient (pre)catalyst for OER and HER

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, FeMoO4 nanoarray and small-sized NGQDs were effectively loaded onto nickel foam via a hydrothermal method, serving as a bifunctional catalyst. We optimized the activities of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) by comparing different quantities of quantum dots, finally identifying an appropriate ratio. Notably, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 demonstrated superior performance in alkaline electrolytes, requiring only 136 and 179 mV overpotential to attain 10 mA cm−2 for HER and OER, respectively. The double electrode composed of this material achieved a voltage of merely 1.57 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The experimental results indicate that Fe3+ serves as the active species for OER, while Mo5+ and oxygen defects on the catalyst surface play a key role in HER. XPS analysis further reveals that the recombination of graphene quantum dots increases the concentration of Fe3+, Mo5+, and oxygen defects. Thus, the recombination of graphene quantum dots is crucial for enhancing catalytic performance. This study offers a novel insight into the design of high-performance bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article (and its supplementary information files).

ce:section.ce:para: The water electrolysis is considered a key solution to the current energy crisis. However, there are still many challenges in the development and practical application of water electrolysis technology. Currently, commercial precious metal catalysts, such as platinum (Pt), exhibit high activity in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [1], while metal oxides like iridium (Ir) and ruthenium dioxide (RuO2) show good activity in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [2]. However, the scarcity and high cost of these precious metal catalysts restrict their widespread application in large-scale hydrogen production [3,4]. Therefore, the utilization of inexpensive and earth-abundant electrocatalysts is highly appealing.

Recently, molybdate salts (CoMoO4 and NiMoO4), serving as inexpensive electrocatalysts, have gained significant attention. The combination of molybdenum (Mo) with first-row transition metals leads to the formation of stable oxides, wherein Mo exists as an oxyanion, Mo4 2-, providing rich redox-active sites [5–12]. Among oxygen evolution catalysts, nickel and cobalt-based catalysts are the focal points of researchers' attention. Iron is commonly utilized as a dopant to enhance the intrinsic activity of the catalyst. However, an increasing number of scientists have observed that iron atoms serve as the true active sites in the OER process [13–15]. Therefore, some researchers have studied the electrolytic properties of FeMoO4 [16–21]. For example, Adak et al. solvothermally deposited FeMoO4 on NF. The catalyst requires only 286 mV overpotential to reach 10 mA cm-2. However, the HER activity of FeMoO4 is poor due to the lack of low-valence state molybdenum [22]. The aforementioned issues can be effectively addressed by combining molybdate salt with carbon materials to enhance the content of low-valence state molybdenum and improve electrical conductivity [23,24]. Among carbon materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained popularity among scholars as electrocatalytic materials due to their small size (<10 nm), high stability, and excellent conductivity. Thus, GQDs can be utilized to form heterogeneous composite nanostructures with transition metal-based materials to obtain high-performance electrocatalysts [25–28]. Therefore, we believed that a compound with FeMoO4 and GQDs would efficiently facilitate overall water splitting. However, this combination has not been explored previously.

In this work, we have successfully fabricated Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 nano-sheet arrays on Ni foam using a two-step hydrothermal method. The results demonstrate that the recombination of graphene quantum dots increases the concentration of low-valence state iron,low-valence state molybdenum, and oxygen defects, thereby enhancing the activity of both hydrogen evolution and oxygen evolution. For instance, to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2 for both HER and OER, the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 requires overpotentials of only 136 mV and 179 mV, respectively. Furthermore, the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1||Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 electrolyzer for water splitting delivered 10 mA cm−2 at a low voltage of 1.57 V and maintained stable operation for over 70 h The superior activity and robust stability demonstrated that theFe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 have promising potential for application in large-scale water splitting.

The reagents we used were all of the analytical grades and were directly utilized without purification. Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Na2MoO4·2H2O, CO(NH2)2 and GQD were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Corporation. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR) and potassium hydroxide (KOH, AR), as well as anhydrous ethanol, were purchased from Liaoning Quan RuiReagent Co. Ltd. Nickel foam was purchased from Hesen Electric Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The commercial graphite rod, Hg/HgO electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode were acquired from AIDA Science-Technology Development Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions. The tailored NF was washed with 3 M HCl and water using ultrasonic treatment to remove unnecessary impurities.

First, 0.1 M Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.1 M Na2MoO4·2H2O, and 0.24 M CO(NH2)2 were dissolved into 30 ml deionized water. Then, the mixture and Ni foam(NF) were transfered to a 50 mL autoclave and conduct a hydrothermal reaction at 100 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, The NF were washed several times with distilled water and ethanol, and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The sample was named as Fe2(MoO4)3
NF.

The dried Fe2(MoO4)3
NF mentioned above was weighed to measure the loading amount of Fe2(MoO4)3. Subsequently, a solution was prepared by adding graphene quantum dots. The mass ratio of Fe2(MoO4)3 to graphene quantum dots was 1:1. Similarly, the Fe2(MoO4)3
NF and graphene quantum dots mixture were kept at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the sample was washed and dried, and named as Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. The preparation methods for Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 were similar to the aforementioned method, and the mass ratio of graphene quantum dots to Fe2(MoO4)3 was different from Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, which was adjusted to 0.5:1 and 2:1, respectively.

SEM images were captured using a Rigaku S-4300 operating. TEM images were obtained using a HITACHI H-7650 transmission electron microscope and Gatan imaging filter (GIF). XPS data were acquired using a Thermo ESCALAB250 X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected at room temperature with a 2θ scan range of 10−90° using an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (1.54 Å).

Electrochemical measurements were conducted in a three-electrode system using an electrochemical station (CHI660E, CH Instrument, USA). The three electrodes included nickel foam as the working electrode (1 cm2), graphite rod, and Hg/HgO (KOH saturated) as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The preparation of the working electrode is described below: The loaded nickel foam material was rinsed with deionized water and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The loading mass of the catalysts was approximately 1 mg/cm2 for all the working electrodes. N2-saturated electrolytes, including 1.0 M KOH, were used to examine the electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts for HER and OER. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scanning rate of 5 mV s-1 was utilized to obtain polarization curves. Chronoamperometry measurements were adopted to assess the long-term stability of the test features. The equation used to calibrate all the measured potentials to RHE is as follows:

Fig. 1 (A) illustrate schematic diagrams of the catalyst synthesis process. Fe2(MoO4)3
NF and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The figure inset of Fig. 1(a) shows the Lowh-resolution-SEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, where a thick layer of nanosheets can be clearly seen loaded on the foam nickel. As shown on Fig. 1(a), the nanosheets are formed by the aggregation of irregular elongated prisms. The height of the prisms is approximately 500 nm. The entire foam nickel is completely covered with interconnected nanosheets, forming patches of nanosheets with an approximate size of 25 μm2 in Fig. 1(a). Fig. 1(b) show the SEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 after the introduction of quantum dots. We can observe that the nanosheets become coarser compared to before. From the inset figure of Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 1(b), it can be observed that the gaps between the nanosheets become smaller after the addition of quantum dots, resulting in a denser and tighter connection overall. Additionally, the elongated prisms on the nanosheets appear more densely packed and compact. As shown in Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 1(b), we also can find that the elongated prisms on the nanosheets become thinner after the addition of quantum dots, while many tooth-like notches are etched out on the prisms by the quantum dots. This sawtooth nanosheets allows for closer contact with water and increases the specific surface area, effectively enhancing its catalytic activity. As a comparison, Fe2(MoO4)3@CQD-NF-0.5 and Fe2(MoO4)3@CQD-NF-2 were prepared by controlling the quantity of graphene quantum dots. The optimal ratio for combining Fe2(MoO4)3 with graphene quantum dots was determined. Fig. S1 shows the SEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2. Fig. S1(a-d) depict the SEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, while Fig. S1(e-h) show the SEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2. The overall structure of the foam nickel nanosheets in Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 is not significantly different from Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 (Fig. S1(a,e)). The main difference lies in the etching of elongated prisms by quantum dots, as shown in Fig. S1(b,f). When the Fe2(MoO4)3/GQD ratio is 1:0.5, due to the lower concentration of quantum dots, the elongated prisms are minimally etched, mostly retaining a smooth state. However, when the ratio reaches 1:2, the excessive concentration of quantum dots causes partial collapse and slimming of the elongated prisms, disrupting the structure of the catalytic material and reducing its catalytic activity. Fig. 1(c, d) depicts the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. In Fig. 1(c), the nano-prismatic structure of approximately 500 nm in length is clearly observed, along with the dot-like features marked by red circles, indicating the successful introduction of graphene quantum dots to the catalyst. Furthermore, Fig. 1(d) reveals the measured interplanar distances of 0.261 nm corresponding to the (123) plane of C and 0.206 nm corresponding to the (214) plane of Fe2(MoO4)3. These values are in accordance with the X-ray diffraction pattern of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 presented in Fig. 2(b). Fig. S2 shows the TEM images of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 (Fig. S2(a-c)) and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 (Fig. S2(d-f)). We can clearly observe their prismatic structures. The nanoprism size of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 is smaller compared to Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5. This is attributed to the heightened concentration of quantum dots, which results in significant etching of the initial Fe2(MoO4)3
NF structure. Elemental mappings indicate that the Mo, Fe, Ni, O, C and N elements are uniformly distributed throughout the nanosheet, suggesting the successful GQD combining to Fe2(MoO4)3 nanosheet (Fig. 1e).

We employed X-ray diffraction (XRD) to investigate the crystal structure and crystallinity of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF. During the XRD tests, we found that X-rays were not able to penetrate the material grown on the nickel foam for transmission analysis. Therefore, the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD structure loaded on the foam nickel was scraped off for further characterization. Fig. S3(a) and Fig. S4(a) show the XRD diffraction patterns of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3
NF respectively. It can be observed that all the peaks can be indexed to the standard card PDF#33–0661 of Fe2(MoO4)3 at 22.8°, 31.0°, and 44.2°, which correspond to the (0 3 1), (3 1 1) and (2 1 4) crystal planes of Fe2(MoO4)3. The characteristic peaks at 14.8°, 22.7°, and 34.2° correspond respectively to the (110), (120), and (123) crystal planes of C(PDF#50–0926) in Fig. S3(a). In Fig. S4(a), the XRD spectrum of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF shows peak positions consistent with Fe2(MoO4)3 (PDF#33–0661), with peaks at 22.8°, 31.0°, and 47.6°, corresponding to the ( 0 3 1 ), ( 3 1 1 ), and ( 2 6 1 ) crystal planes of Fe2(MoO4)3, respectively. The increase in XRD peak intensity after the addition of quantum dots indicates an enhancement in crystallinity. The increase in crystallinity signifies an increase in the material's orderliness or a decrease in lattice defects, which is beneficial for enhancing catalytic activity.

Fig. S3(b) displays the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. The maximum melting point of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 occurred at 342.8 °C, attributed to the presence of GQDs on its surface. The Raman spectra of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts are shown in Fig. S3(c), where the D band at around 1325 cm-1 and the G band at around 1584 cm-1 are attributed to the structural defects on the graphite plane and the E2 g vibration mode corresponding to the sp2 bonded graphite carbon, respectively. The intensity ratio of d-band and G-band (ID/IG) reveals the degree of graphitization. Clearly, the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalyst demonstrates a higher G band intensity and a lower value of ID/IG, indicating a higher degree of graphitization. Typically, a higher degree of graphitization enhances the electrical conductivity of the carbon skeleton. Therefore, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, with a higher degree of graphitization should facilitate faster electron transfer.

We investigated the electronic interactions and chemical valence states of the prepared core material using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). All XPS spectra were calibrated using the C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV In Fig. S3(d), four peaks are observed in the C 1 s spectrum of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, located at 284.6, 283.1, 286.5, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to the functional groups of CC/C = C, CH, CN and OC = O, respectively [29]. Fig. S3(e) represents the N 1 s spectrum of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, revealing a single peak at 397.9 eV [30], indicating the presence of pyridinic nitrogen. Fig. S3(f) exhibits the XPS spectrum of the O 1 s orbitals, showing two peaks at 530.3 eV and 531.5 eV [31], corresponding to lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen vacancies, respectively. The presence of lattice oxygen and oxygen vacancies suggests the presence of defect structures in Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, which can impact its performance. In crystal structures, lattice oxygen refers to oxygen atoms occupying lattice points that adhere precisely to the crystal structure rules. Oxygen vacancies, on the other hand, refer to unoccupied positions within the crystal where oxygen atoms are absent. Lattice oxygen and oxygen vacancies can serve as active sites or defect positions, participating in charge transfer, ion diffusion, and other reaction processes, thereby influencing the electrochemical performance of the material. Consequently, the electrochemical performance of this material is enhanced.

The Ni 2p XPS spectrum of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 exhibits two peaks at 855.7 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 873.2 eV (Ni 2p1/2), indicating the oxidation state of Ni2+. Additionally, two satellite peaks at 861.3 eV (Ni 2p3/2 sat) and 879.3 eV (Ni 2p1/2 sat) [32] are observed (Fig. S3(g)), which are likely a result of exposure to air. In Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, Ni2+ acts as a common catalyst and provides active sites for reactant adsorption, facilitating electron transfer in electrochemical reactions. Its presence enhances the rate and efficiency of electrochemical reactions, thereby promoting the electrocatalytic performance of the material. Ni2+ demonstrates excellent electrochemical stability and can maintain stability across various electrochemical environments. It actively participates in redox reactions and aids in electron transfer during electrochemical processes. This redox capability can be harnessed in energy conversion and storage devices like batteries and electrolyzers, offering adjustable redox reactions. The XPS spectrum depicted in Fig. S3(h) illustrates the Fe 2p orbitals of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. Two peaks at 710.3 eV (Fe3+ 2p3/2) and 723.8 eV (Fe3+ 2p1/2) indicate the presence of Fe3+-O [33]. Fe3+ serves as an additional catalytically active site, facilitating the adsorption of reactant species and promoting electron transfer pathways. This phenomenon enhances the rate and efficiency of electrochemical reactions. Due to its higher oxidation state, Fe3+ can accept or donate electrons during redox reactions, thereby facilitating the progress of electrochemical reactions. The XPS spectrum illustrated in Fig. S3(i) displays the Mo 3d orbitals of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. Two peaks at 230.9 eV (Mo5+ 3d5/2) and 234.1 eV (Mo5+ 3d3/2) indicate the presence of Mo5+ [34]. Additionally, two peaks at 232.1 eV (Mo6+ 3d5/2) and 235.3 eV (Mo6+ 3d3/2) [35] are assigned to the Mo6+ oxidation state. In electrochemical oxidation reactions, the presence of Mo in the +6 oxidation state can act as a catalyst. It actively participates in the reaction and facilitates electron transfer pathways, effectively increasing the reaction rate. This contributes to enhanced electrochemical catalytic activity and stability of the material. The presence of Mo6+ has a significant positive influence on electrochemical processes. However, it is noteworthy that Mo in the +5 oxidation state can also play a role in promoting electrochemical reactions.

The XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Ni 2p, Fe 2p, and Mo 3d for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF are depicted in Fig. S4(b-g). The notable differences between Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3
NF are the percentage of Fe3+, Mo5+, CH bonds. The appearance of CH bonds subsequent to the addition of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is commonly associated with the presence of surface functional groups. When GQDs undergo chemical modification or functionalization, some carbon atoms may form CH bonds with hydrogen atoms. The introduction of these functional groups can alter the surface properties of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, thereby enhancing their reactivity and selectivity in chemical reactions. The presence of CH bonds provides active sites for other functional groups to react, enhancing the capability for the adsorption and activation of reactants. The negative shift in the pyridine nitrogen upon the addition of GQDs may be attributed to the influence of functional groups or substituents with higher electron density near the nitrogen atom. This higher electron density functional group is the carbon-hydrogen (CH) group. This further confirms the effective doping of graphene quantum dots. The Mo5+ in Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is due to the potential occurrence of charge transfer or electron transfer reactions when graphene quantum dots (GQDs) interact with Mo6+. The complete substitution of Fe3+ for Fe0+ may also be related to the recombination of carbon quantum dots. The recombination of carbon quantum dots increases the content of Fe3+ and Mo5+ respectively, and then promotes the number of active sites for oxygen evolution and hydrogen evolution at the same time.

The HER performance of various catalysts loaded on foam nickel was evaluated in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode setup with a scanning rate of 5 mV s-1. Electrochemical tests were conducted on Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF (0.5:1 / 1:1 / 2:1) under different ratios to compare their performances, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) presents the HER polarization curves obtained through LSV measurements with infrared correction. At a current density of 10 mA cm-2, the overpotentials for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 were recorded as 179, 191, 136, and 162 mV, respectively. The results shown in Fig. 2(a) demonstrate that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 exhibits the best performance in the HER. Additionally, it outperforms many of the currently developed catalysts, as indicated in Table S1.

To investigate the kinetics of the HER for Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, Tafel plots were constructed using the current density (j) and overpotential (η) data depicted in Fig. 2(a) for |j| ≤ 10 mA cm-2. The Tafel equation, η = b log |j| + a, was applied to derive the slopes of these plots, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(b), where 'b' signifies the Tafel slope, and 'a' is a constant term. The Tafel plots for Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and its corresponding control groups are presented in the figure. According to Fig. 2(b), the Tafel slope of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 (75.9 mV dec‑1) is comparatively smaller than that of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 (82.1 mV dec‑1), Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 (117.6 mV dec‑1), and Fe2(MoO4)3
NF (118.5 mV dec‑1). A reduced Tafel slope suggests an accelerated kinetic rate. By comparing these values with the standard Tafel slope, potential mechanisms can be inferred as follows:

The observed Tafel slopes of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are closer to 40 mV dec‑1, indicating that the rate-determining step in their reactions is likely the Heyrovsky reaction. Conversely, the Tafel slope values of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 and Fe2(MoO4)3
NF are closer to 120 mV dec‑1, suggesting that the rate-determining step in the reactions of these two catalysts is the Volmer reaction..

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data at η HER = 200 mV were measured to further study the OER kinetics of these catalyst. Fig. 2(c) illustrates the Nyquist plots of these catalysts. The impedance spectra were fitted using an equivalent circuit composed of (L1 + R1 + Q2/R2 + Q3/R3), where R1, R2, and R3 represent resistances, and Q2 and Q3 are constant phase elements associated with the materials. L1 represents the inductance connecting the cables. The disparity between R3 and R1 is denoted as the charge transfer resistance (R ct), which influences ion transport during the reaction process. A lower R ct value signifies better HER activity. The RCT values of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and its control groups are depicted in the figure. Among the synthesized samples, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5 exhibited a higher RCT value of 1.09 Ω, while Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 displayed the lowest charge transfer resistance (RCT) value of 0.58 Ω. Another sample, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2, demonstrated an impedance value of 0.86 Ω. The impedance of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF is 1.26 Ω. These findings suggest that the incorporation of graphene quantum dots enhances ion transport in Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, thereby improving its HER kinetics.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of a catalyst plays a vital role in its catalytic activity for the HER. The ECSA is directly related to the double-layer capacitance (C dl), which can be determined by measuring the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves in the non-Faradaic potential region (Fig. S5). As depicted in Fig. S5(f), the C dl values for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 were found to be 0.9, 0.9, 1.1, and 0.8 mF cm-2. Therefore, we can calculate that the specific capacitance per square centimeter of the nickel foam based material we used is 24 μF/cm2. Consequently, the ECSA value can be converted by C dl through the following equation:
(7)
ECSA =
C
dl
( mF
cm − 2 ) / 0
. 024
( mF
cm − 2 )

The calculated ECSA values for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are 37.5, 37.5, 45.8, and 33.3, respectively. Based on these results, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 exhibits the highest ECSA among the compared electrocatalysts. From this result, we found that by controlling the ratio of Fe2(MoO4)3 and graphene quantum dots can enhance the number of active sites and achieve a larger active surface area. Consequently, this contributes significantly to its excellent catalytic activity for HER by facilitating efficient transfer channels and rapid electron transfer. Therefore, it can be concluded that the ratio of Fe2(MoO4)3 loading amount to graphene quantum dot incorporation is an important factor influencing the catalytic activity of our material.

To gain an insight into the intrinsic HER activity, the TOF values for the HER electrocatalysts were calculated using the following equation:
(8)
TOF
=

×

) / ( 2
×

Here, J represents the HER current density, A denotes the surface area of the anode, and F stands for the Faraday constant. The number of active sites (n) was determined using cyclic voltammetry (pH = 7) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. As shown in Fig. S6, at the same overpotential, the TOFs of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 are significantly higher than those of other catalysts. At a voltage of −0.2 V, the TOF values for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are −1.29194 s-1, −0.82013 s-1, −2.32824 s-1, and −2.28162 s-1, respectively.

Stability is another crucial factor to consider for water electrolysis catalysts. To assess the long-term stability of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalyst. The stability measurements were conducted at a overpotential of 268 mV. As shown in Fig. 2(d), after 50 h of measurement in an alkaline medium, the current density of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 decreased from 50.3 mA cm-2 to 50.26 mA cm-2. This result further emphasizes the HER stability of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalyst and indicates its potential for stable HER performance.

The structure of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalyst after the HER process at η HER = 268 mV for 1 h was characterized by XPS. We compared the XPS spectra of samples that before and after HER process (Fig. S7). Fig. S7(a) shows the XPS spectrum of C 1s. By comparing the spectra, we observed the disappearance of the CH bond and a negative shift of 0.9 eV in the CN bond. Additionally, the OC = O bond exhibited a positive shift of 0.6 eV Furthermore, the peak area of the CN bond showed a significant increase. Following a prolonged stability test, the absence of the CH bond implies the release or conversion of hydrogen atoms into other compounds during the reaction. This suggests that the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 electrode exhibits excellent catalytic activity and can effectively facilitate hydrogen production or other hydrogen-related reactions [36]. The negative shift observed in the CN bond could be ascribed to alterations in the chemical environment surrounding nitrogen atoms. Such changes might stem from structural modifications within the material or reactions involving nitrogen atoms occurring during the stability test. This phenomenon is likely influenced by factors like adsorption on the catalyst surface and reactant activation during the electrolytic water splitting process. The positive shift observed in the OC = O bond suggests a change in the interaction between the material and oxygen atoms. This modification could be linked to catalyst surface oxidation triggered by the stability test or reactions involving oxygen. The positive shift may signify a stronger interaction between oxygen atoms and the catalyst, potentially enhancing catalytic activity and stability. The notable increase in the peak area of the CN bond implies the formation of additional CN bonds on the surface following the stability test [37]. This heightened peak area of the CN bond could indicate the preservation or enhancement of active sites on the material's surface, thereby enhancing the catalyst's activity and stability. In Fig. S7(b), the XPS spectrum of N 1 s is displayed, revealing a positive shift of 0.5 eV in pyridinic nitrogen, along with an additional peak at 396.6 eV corresponding to NNi/Mo bonding. The positive shift in pyridinic nitrogen could be ascribed to an enhanced interaction between the surface nitrogen atoms of the material and metals like Ni and Mo induced by the stability test. This interaction may foster a closer bonding between the nitrogen atoms in the pyridinic ring and the metals, thereby resulting in a positive shift in their electronic energy levels [38]. The presence of the NNi/Mo peak indicates an increased occurrence of bonding between the material surface and nickel or molybdenum. In Fig. S7(c), the XPS spectrum of O1 s is depicted, revealing a positive shift of 0.3 eV in the O-lattice bond at 530.3 eV, while the oxygen vacancy (O-V) peak is absent or has disappeared. The positive change in the O-lattice bond could be attributed to an augmented interaction between the oxygen atoms and the Ni/Mo atoms within the lattice. This heightened interaction may result in a closer bonding between the oxygen atoms and the Ni/Mo atoms, leading to a positive shift in their electronic energy levels. Additionally, this enhanced interaction could contribute to the disappearance or filling of oxygen vacancies within the lattice.

Fig. S7(d) shows the XPS spectrum of Ni 2P, and there are no significant changes. The two characteristic peaks of Ni2+ have undergone a negative shift of approximately 0.2 eV Conversely, the satellite peaks have experienced a positive change of around 0.6 eV This could be attributed to the heightened interaction between Ni2+ and other components of the catalys. The XPS spectrum of Fe 2p in Fig. S7(e) displays a negative shift of 0.2 eV in the Fe3+-O bond located at 710.3 eV and 723.8 eV Additionally, two new characteristic peaks, Fe3+-Mo 2p3/2 and Fe3+-Mo 2p1/2, emerge at 711.8 eV and 725.6 eV, respectively. From this observation, the change in O 1 s (Fig. S7(c)) may be attributed to the enhanced interaction between O and Fe3+ [34]. Indeed, the appearance of Fe3+-Mo is a result of the reinforced interaction between Fe3+ and Mo in the catalyst. Therefore, it can be inferred that the presence of Fe is a crucial factor contributing to the enhanced catalytic activity in the catalyst. The XPS spectrum of Mo 3d in Fig. S7(f) indicates that the two characteristic peaks of Mo6+ have undergone a negative shift of approximately 0.2 eV, while Mo5+ has experienced a positive shift of 0.3 eV This phenomenon may be attributed to the reinforced interaction between Fe3+ and Mo [39]. The enhanced interaction between Fe3+ and Mo could indeed lead to the negative shift of the Mo6+ characteristic peak and the positive shift of the Mo5+ characteristic peak. This observation further underscores the significance of Fe in augmenting the catalytic activity of the catalyst.

The OER activities of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 can be observed from the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). shown in Fig. 3(a) , at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, the overpotentials for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are 182, 216, 179, and 274 mV, respectively. Similarly, at a current density of 20 mA cm-2, the overpotentials for Fe2(MoO4)3
NF and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 are 228 and 199 mV, respectively. This result indicate that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 demonstrates excellent OER activity, showcasing significant improvement compared to the Fe2(MoO4)3
NF precursor. Additionally, it demonstrates favorable performance across various ratios. This suggests that the addition of graphene quantum dots enhances the OER activity of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, making it a promising catalyst for oxygen evolution. Compared to most catalysts available on the market, catalyst Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 exhibits superior performance. For example, Table S2 provides a comparison of its performance against other catalysts.

The Tafel plot was used to evaluating the electrochemical kinetics of the OER for Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF. The Tafel slopes of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are 45.9, 117.7, 65.4, and 54.8 mV dec‑1, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). In this case, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 exhibits the smallest Tafel slope, indicating a faster reaction kinetics compared to the other materials. This suggests that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 has a more efficient catalytic activity for the OER. Futhermore, the Tafel slope of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is very close to 40 mV dec‑1 , revealing that the second electron transfer step is the rate-determining step. However, The Tafel slopes of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF is close to 120 mV dec‑1 , suggesting that the first electron transfer step in a sequential reaction is the rate-determining step. This result indicated that the recombination of carbon quantum dots can change the OER kinetics mechanism.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data at η OER = 300 mV were measured to further study the OER kinetics of these catalysts. In Fig. 3(c), The impedance values of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are as follows: 0.57 Ω, 1.17 Ω, 0.98 Ω and 0.63 Ω. The Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 have the lowest charge transfer resistance (R CT) compared to the other materials. A lower RCT value is favorable for efficient electron transport during the OER. Therefore, the introduction of GQD reduces the charge-transfer resistance of catalysts, thus facilitating an improvement of the OER kinetics.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of a catalyst plays a vital role in its catalytic activity for the OER. The ECSA is directly related to the double-layer capacitance (C dl), which can be determined by measuring the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves in the non-Faradaic potential region (Fig. S8). From Fig. S8(f), it is evident that Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 exhibit C dl values of 1.0 mF/cm2, 1.1 mF/cm2, 1.4 mF/cm2, and 1.2 mF/cm2, respectively. Based on the Eq. (7), we can calculate the ECSA values of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2, which are: 41.7, 45.8, 58.3 and 50.0. The largest ECSA of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 indicated that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 have an bigger reaction contact area and more efficient electron transfer capability. Consequently, this leads to significantly improved OER catalytic activity.

To gain an insight into the intrinsic OER activity, the TOF values for the OER electrocatalysts were calculated using the following equation:
(9)
TOF
=

×

) / ( 4
×
n
×
F )

As shown in Fig. S9, at a fixed voltage of 1.3 V, the TOFs of Fe2(MoO4)3
NF, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-0.5, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-2 are 0.04358 s-1, 0.0798 s-1, 0.26941 s-1 and 0.03961 s-1, respectively. It is evident that the TOF value of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is the highest. Therefore, we can conclude that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 has highest intrinsic activity in the OER reaction compared to other catalysts.

Stability is also an important factor for the water electrolysis of these catalysts. Fig. 3(d) provides a clear demonstration of the exceptional stability of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 in the OER. The long-term stability of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalyst was assessed at a given overpotential of 216 mV. The current density increased from 50.24 to 50.28 mA m-2 after a testing period of up to 50 h This result indicates the remarkable long-term stability of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 in alkaline media during the OER reaction. This long-term stability demonstrates its suitability for practical applications that require sustained OER activity.

As shown in Fig. S10, the surface valence states of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts undergo obvious changes before and after the OER process. Fig. S10(a) shows the XPS spectra of C 1s. Similar to HER, the CH bond disappeared, the CN bond exhibited a negative shift and increased in intensity, and the OC = O bond showed a positive shift. These observations indicate that similar changes occurred as in HER reaction, suggesting good electrocatalytic activity of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1. Fig. S10(b) presents the N 1 s spectrum. Interestingly, after OER stability testing, there was the appearance of purine nitrogen, possibly due to its involvement in charge transfer and intermediate formation during oxygen reduction reaction, thereby promoting the OER activity of the catalyst. As depicted in Fig. S10(c), the O 1 s spectrum exhibited that the O-lattice bonds disappeared. This occurrence may be attributed to the reaction of O atoms with other reactants to generate O2 [40], which then leaves the catalyst surface, thus forming a large number of oxygen vacancies. In Fig. S10(d), the XPS spectrum of Ni 2p shown little change except for a slight positive shift. However, a fraction of Fe3+ was reduced to Fe0 (Fig. S10(e)). Such reduction could occur during long-term redox reactions. Finally, Fig. S10(f) presents a before-and-after comparison of the Mo 3d XPS spectra, clearly showing the disappearance of Mo5+ [41], which may be due to oxidation reactions leading to the loss of electrons by Mo ions, resulting in their conversion to a higher oxidation state. Considering all factors, the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 electrocatalyst maintained its performance even after 50 h of OER stability testing, demonstrating its beneficial stability properties. These XPS results indicate that the surfaces of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts after the OER are mainly composed of Fe0, Fe3+, Ni2+, Mo6+, oxygen vacancies, and N species. Thus, the main active phase of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts toward the OER is Fe3+, and the Graphene quantum dots play an important role in adsorption and enhancement of electrical conductivity.

The above electrochemical measurement results indicate that the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts exhibit high activity and robust stability towards both the HER and OER. Thus, we assembled a twoelectrode configuration using the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 catalysts as the cathode and the anode (Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1|Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1) for full water splitting, and Pt|IrO2 (loaded on NF) as the experimental control group. The full water splitting was carried out between 1 and 2 V in 1.0 M KOH at room temperature with a scan rate of 5 mV s-1. In Fig. 4(a) , the cell(Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1|Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1) voltage required to drive a current density of 10 mA cm2 is ca 1.57 V. This indicates that its catalytic water oxidation and reduction combined overpotential is only 388 mV. Additionally, thevoltage of Pt|IrO2 is 1.55 V. However, at a current density of 20 mA/cm2, the voltage of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is on par with that of Pt and RuO2, and the voltage is 1 .62V. At higher current densities, the overpotential of catalyst Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is lower than that of the dual-electrode composed of Pt and RuO2. This demonstrating the excellent bifunctional characteristics of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 electrocatalyst material. Moreover, the exceptional long-term stability of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 for dual-function water splitting is evident in Fig. 4(b). Even after 70 h of continuous reaction at a current density of 50 mA cm-2, no significant decay in performance is observed. This underscores the outstanding stability of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 during prolonged electrocatalytic processes. The superior dual-functionality of the Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 material can be attributed to the synergistic catalytic activity facilitated by the composite of Ni, Fe, and Mo multi-metal sites, along with the effective enhancement achieved when loaded on nickel foam. Furthermore, the integration of graphene quantum dots contributes to the availability of additional active sites. In conclusion, the synthesis of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 offers an efficient means for harnessing dual-functional catalysis in water electrolysis. Its remarkable catalytic activity, stability, and unique material composition make it a promising candidate for various electrochemical applications.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a novel foam nickel composite material, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1, using a two-step hydrothermal method. From various test results, it is evident that Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 is an efficient, cost-effective, and stable bifunctional electrocatalyst. The interaction between the three metal elements, Fe, Ni, and Mo, enhances the conductivity and stability of the catalytic material, while significantly reducing the production cost compared to noble metals like Pt and RuO2. Subsequently, the addition of graphene quantum dots increases the active surface area of the nanomaterial in contact with water, further enhancing the electrochemical performance of the catalyst. The bifunctional electrode system composed of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 achieves a current density of 10 mA cm-2 with an overpotential of only 1.57 V in 1 M KOH electrolyte. It also exhibits remarkable stability during a 70 h durability test, showing no significant performance degradation. Through the electrocatalytic process, we have reconstructed the structure of Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 and found that Fe-Mo, Fe-O, and pyridinic N are likely the true active species involved in the alkaline HER and OER processes. Based on the above findings, Fe2(MoO4)3@GQD-NF-1 nanosheets as catalysts exhibit great potential for large-scale water splitting applications.
